=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of burial mounds, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>

<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>

<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.

# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around burial mounds during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on burial mounds followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of burial mounds, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of burial mounds, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Burial Mounds and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>

<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>

In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

revision.text:
[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around burial mounds during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on burial mounds followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of burial mounds, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of burial mounds, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Burial Mounds and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>

<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>

<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>

In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. [[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around burial mounds during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on burial mounds followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of burial mounds, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of burial mounds, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on burial mounds followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of burial mounds, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of burial mounds, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.

The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />

=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.

<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>

=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "ompletion tier" to "]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:not indeterminate]][[Category:"
revision.text:
[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, qq. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa aqqear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|qq=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|qq=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Aqqearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
