[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Completion tier 6]] to [[Category:Completion tier 7]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.

There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:qq]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Figural Haniwa]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Figural Haniwa]][[Category:New]]

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, 11. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa a11ear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|11=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|11=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - A11earance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear[[Category:pp to pp]] from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.

=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

revision.text:
[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|жҹідә•иҢ¶иҮјеұұеҸӨеўі}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.
== Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}
The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear[[Category:pp to pp]] from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" />
== Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}.
=== House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>
=== Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|crowns|ja|еҶ }}, {{ill|chairs|ja|жӨ…еӯҗ}}, containers like {{ill|tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә}}, and weapons like {{ill|swords|ja|еӨ§еҲҖ}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}

The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|йҮҚиҰҒж–ҮеҢ–иІЎ}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Figural Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}.

There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010е№ҙ}}

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|ja|е®®еҙҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saito|ja|иҘҝйғҪеёӮ}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|жқұдә¬еӣҪз«ӢеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}} collection. </gallery>

These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals.
<gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|жқҫйҳӘеёӮ}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|дёүйҮҚзңҢ}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery>
=== Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles.
<gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|ja|еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІжӯҰдәә}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
=== Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң}}, and {{ill|boars|ja|гӮӨгғҺгӮ·гӮ·}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time.
<gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya City|ja|зҶҠи°·еёӮ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery>
== Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}.
== Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages:
# '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|еҹҙијӘ}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum|ja|иҠқеұұеҸӨеўігғ»гҒҜгҒ«гӮҸеҚҡзү©йӨЁ}}
