They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[:en:Junshi|Junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[:en:Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[:en:Junshi|Junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[:en:Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[:en:Junshi|Junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[:en:Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11487889}} [[Category:Animals in art]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:Haniwa]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[:en:Junshi|Junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[:en:Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

[[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11487889}} [[Category:Animals in art]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:Haniwa]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]], earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[:en:Junshi|Junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[:en:Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] in the [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]]) - [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including [[:en:Crown|crowns]], [[:en:Chair|chairs]], containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like [[:en:еӨ§еҲҖ|swords]], bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: [[:en:Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor]] (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and [[:en:Wild boar|boars]] are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from [[:en:Kumagaya|Kumagaya City]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Cluster|Shibayama Kofun Cluster]] in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi}} (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi}}'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}} (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi region}} through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|WD=Q850479|lt=Saito}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|Crown|en|Crown|WD=Q170984|lt=crowns}}, {{ill|Chair|en|Chair|WD=Q15026|lt=chairs}}, containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like {{ill|еӨ§еҲҖ|en|еӨ§еҲҖ|WD=Q1224104|lt=swords}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|en|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|WD=Q91239334|lt=Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and {{ill|Wild boar|en|Wild boar|WD=Q58697|lt=boars}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya|en|Kumagaya|WD=Q41106|lt=Kumagaya City}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|en|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11614635|lt=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|en|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|WD=Q11614636|lt=Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11487889}} [[Category:Animals in art]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:Haniwa]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Representational haniwa]][[ja:еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11487889]] Wikidata name: representational haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 3 Haniwa.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|en|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|WD=Q11534089|lt=Yanai Chausuyama Kofun}} figurative haniwa: house-shaped haniwa (foreground) and lid-shaped haniwa (left background).]] [[File:еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўі - panoramio.jpg|thumb|250px|Figurative haniwa arranged on the forward section of Tsumakawari Kofun No. 4, restored for display. The artifacts were excavated in {{ill|ЕҢta, Gunma|ja|зҫӨйҰ¬зңҢеӨӘз”°еёӮ|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢta, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>[https://www.city.ota.gunma.jp/005gyosei/0170-009kyoiku-bunka/bunkazai/otabunka47.html еЎҡе»»гӮҠеҸӨеўізҫӨз¬¬4еҸ·еҸӨеўі] - Ota City, accessed April 21, 2020.</ref>]] '''Figurative haniwa''' ({{lang|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|1=Representational Haniwa}}, ''keishЕҚ haniwa'') are {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}}, earthenware objects erected on or around Kofuns during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. These haniwa depict houses, vessels, people, and animals in specific and symbolic forms. They contrast with {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}, which include the {{ill|morning glory-shaped haniwa|ja|жңқйЎ”еҪўеҹҙијӘ|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning glory-shaped haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}}. == Mythical Origins in the Kiki == The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi}} (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.<ref>It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition.</ref> Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi}}'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika}} (646).{{Sfn|еҖӯеҪҰе‘ҪпјҲеҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—Ҹпјү|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|еҪўиұЎеҹҙијӘ|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}).<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ">[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 20pp, pp. 77-78.</ref> Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi region}} through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="жЈ®жө©дёҖ" /> == Types == Figurative haniwa are broadly classified into four categories: '''house-shaped haniwa''', '''vessel-shaped haniwa''', '''human-shaped haniwa''', and '''animal-shaped haniwa'''. Their placement on Kofuns followed specific arrangements {{Sfn|Takahashi|2008|pp=101-135}}. === House-Shaped Haniwa === House-shaped haniwa were centrally placed at the tops of Kofuns, serving as a focal point. They were rarely positioned alone, often surrounded by cylindrical haniwa and vessel-shaped haniwa. These depictions are valuable for reconstructing buildings such as residences of tribal chiefs, shrines, or ritual spaces. <gallery> иҘҝйғҪеҺҹеҸӨеўізҫӨеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ еӯҗжҢҒе®¶.JPG|House with figures (excavated from {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|WD=Q850479|lt=Saito}}) - {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} collection. </gallery> === Vessel-Shaped Haniwa === These haniwa, positioned to protect house-shaped haniwa, depict various items such as lids, ceremonial umbrellas, and insignia of prestige, including {{ill|Crown|en|Crown|WD=Q170984|lt=crowns}}, {{ill|Chair|en|Chair|WD=Q15026|lt=chairs}}, containers like {{ill|Shingu|lt=Shingu|lt=Tall stands|ja|й«ҳеқҸ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tall stands|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to зҘһе…·}} and {{ill|pots|ja|еЈә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pots|1=pots|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q61055332}}, and weapons like {{ill|еӨ§еҲҖ|en|еӨ§еҲҖ|WD=Q1224104|lt=swords}}, bows, and armor. These objects are crucial for reconstructing funerary rituals. <gallery> е®қеЎҡ1еҸ·еўіеҮәеңҹ иҲ№еҪўеҹҙијӘ (1еҸ·иҲ№).JPG|Boat-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)|lt=Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|ja|е®қеЎҡеҸӨеўі|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}) - Matsusaka City collection. </gallery> === Human-Shaped Haniwa === Human-shaped haniwa were placed in outlying sections of Kofuns, including depictions of leaders, shamans, musicians, warriors, and common folk. These representations provide insight into costumes, social hierarchies, and ceremonial roles. <gallery> еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”әеҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ жҢӮз”ІгҒ®жӯҰдәә-2.JPG|[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: {{ill|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|en|Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor|WD=Q91239334|lt=Haniwa Warrior in Keiko Armor}} (excavated from {{ill|Iizuka, Ota City|ja|еӨӘз”°еёӮйЈҜеЎҡз”ә|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuka, Ota City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> === Animal-Shaped Haniwa === Animal-shaped haniwa, especially horses, emphasized the authority of the deceased. Other animals such as {{ill|dogs|ja|зҠ¬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dogs|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮӨгғҢ|1=dog|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q144}}, {{ill|birds|ja|йөң|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/birds|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to гӮҰз§‘|1=Phalacrocoracidae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3901247}}, and {{ill|Wild boar|en|Wild boar|WD=Q58697|lt=boars}} are also represented, reflecting the human-animal relationships of the time. <gallery> еҹјзҺүзңҢзҶҠи°·еёӮдёҠдёӯжқЎж—Ҙеҗ‘еі¶еҮәеңҹ еҹҙијӘ йҰ¬-2.JPG|Horse-shaped haniwa (excavated from {{ill|Kumagaya|en|Kumagaya|WD=Q41106|lt=Kumagaya City}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}) - Tokyo National Museum collection. </gallery> == Related Research == Research on figurative haniwa has evolved since the early 20th century. In 1931, Morikazu Goto's work introduced interpretations linking these haniwa to funerary ceremonies {{Sfn|Goto|1931|pp=26-50}}. Subsequent excavations, such as those at {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|en|Shibayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11614635|lt=Shibayama Kofun Cluster}} in Chiba Prefecture, revealed detailed haniwa arrangements, contributing to an understanding of their ceremonial contexts {{Sfn|Otsuka et al.|1989|p=487}}. == Changes Over Time == The evolution of figurative haniwa can be divided into four stages: # '''Stage 1''': Early 4th century - Initial use of house-shaped haniwa at mound summits. # '''Stage 2''': Late 4th century - Introduction of haniwa groupings on mounded terraces. # '''Stage 3''': Mid-5th to mid-6th century - Appearance of more diverse figures and ritual arrangements. # '''Stage 4''': Late 6th century - Simplification and reduction in types of figurative haniwa. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="жіЁ"}} == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == See Also == * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} * {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|еҶҶзӯ’еҹҙијӘ|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|en|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|WD=Q11614636|lt=Shibayama Kofuns and Haniwa Museum}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
